≈õukasaptati_o.Title: ≈öukasaptati, ‚ÄúTextus Ornatior‚Äù
Edition: Series: Abhandlungen der Philosophisch-Philologischen Classe der K√∂niglich Bayerischen Akademie der Wissenschaften
Extent: The complete work is nearly extant except for a few small lacunae.
The edition contains a complete text of the known work.
The text fills 66 pages in the edition (pp. 319‚Äì384), with no other intervening material.
≈õukasaptati_o.Structure: The work consists of seventy stories in a frame story. The frame story's introduction and conclusion are explicitly distinguished by the edition as _kathƒÅvatƒÅra_ (9pp in edition) and _kathƒÅvadhi_ (1p), respectively.
≈õukasaptati_o.Work Description: The work's frame story takes place over seventy nights. In the frame, a pet parrot belongs to a young, lonely woman named PrabhƒÅvatƒ´, and he seeks to keep her faithful while her merchant husband, Madanasena (cp. Madanavinoda in the _simplicior_ text), is away on business. Each night, when PrabhƒÅvatƒ´ is tempted to meet a lover, the clever bird tells her a story meant to dissuade her. After seventy nights of such tales, her husband returns, and she has remained true, bringing the drama to a close. The stories feature clever protagonists who extricate themselves from difficult situations, especially women who outsmart men in episodes involving adultery.
Digitization Notes: (Aug 2022) Suhas Mahesh (Ambuda user `suhasm`): Item created, Google Cloud Vision OCR performed.
(May‚ÄìJul 2025) Tyler Neill: Second OCR and merge with previous corrections, proofread final time, restructured for HANSEL.
≈õukasaptati_o.File Creation Method: I (Tyler Neill) produced this HANSEL edition by first extracting the text page-by-page from the Ambuda website ‚ÄúProofing‚Äù section. I then ran a PDF of the edition through both Google Cloud Vision OCR (via Skrutable) and Gemini 2.0 Flash (via Dharmamitra). These two OCR outputs were then automatically harmonized using Gemini 2.5 Pro (via direct API and using a custom prompt). This harmonized OCR output was then contrasted against the Ambuda-derived version using Meld and differences were reconciled individually with reference to the PDF. I then fully proofread the text, word by word. Finally, I contributed content improvements back to Ambuda using its page-by-page Proofing interface.

≈õukasaptati_s.Structure: The work consists of seventy stories in a frame story. The edition does not explicitly distinguish the frame story's introduction and conclusion with distinct sections.
≈õukasaptati_s.Work Description: The work's frame story takes place over seventy nights. In the frame, a pet parrot belongs to a young, lonely woman named PrabhƒÅvatƒ´, and he seeks to keep her faithful while her merchant husband, Madanavinoda, is away on business. Each night, when PrabhƒÅvatƒ´ is tempted to meet a lover, the clever bird tells her a story meant to dissuade her. After seventy nights of such tales, her husband returns, and she has remained true, bringing the drama to a close. The stories feature clever protagonists who extricate themselves from difficult situations, especially women who outsmart men in episodes involving adultery.
Translations: See excellent translation bibliography by V. Gostishchev, [on Google Drive (Word doc)](https://docs.google.com/document/d/17vAjnnIo5j-yd1gcq9xBIFlpHCzyRTW-/edit?usp=drive_link&ouid=109139093909110592902&rtpof=true&sd=true)

≈õukasaptati_s.File Creation Method: I (Tyler Neill) produced this HANSEL edition by manually converting the original Word document to plaintext and then automatically adding XML markup. The text still needs a final proofread.

Additional Files.description: Overview of translations by V. Gostishchev

Additional Notes: Indian editions (e.g. Ramakant Tripathi, VaÃÑraÃÑnÃ£asiÃÑ, 1966, basis of the GRETIL text) are based on this _Simplicior_ text by Schmidt.
V. Gostishchev's original version of the file contains added _chƒÅyƒÅs_ from the Indian editions and other useful notes.
On the construction of the text, see the editor's 1890 PhD dissertation ‚ÄúVier Erz√§hlungen aus der √áukasaptati‚Äù, [on Google Books](https://books.google.fr/books?id=iq5LAQAAMAAJ)
See also the editor's: ‚ÄúAnmerkungen zu dem Textus simplicior der ≈öukasaptati‚Äù, ZDMG 48, 1894, 580-628.

kumƒÅrilabha·π≠·π≠a_≈õlokavƒÅrtika.Work Description: ≈ölokavƒÅrttika is one of the foundational texts of classical Indian philosophy, especially for understanding how Sanskrit intellectuals argued about knowledge, language, and authority. In it, KumƒÅrila argues that Vedic language is a reliable means for knowing what human beings ought to do, not because of the intention of any human or divine author, but because of stable, conventional relations inherent in language itself. Though formally a commentary on parts of Jaimini‚Äôs Mƒ´mƒÅ·πÉsƒÅs≈´tra, the work functions less as explanation than as intertextual polemic, sharpening debates with Buddhists, NaiyƒÅyikas, and rival Mƒ´mƒÅ·πÉsakas in tightly compressed verse. For getting started with this notoriously difficult text, the most reliable English-language resources are the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy articles on Mƒ´mƒÅ·πÉsƒÅ and Testimony (≈õabda), together with John Taber‚Äôs essays on KumƒÅrila and svata·∏• prƒÅmƒÅ·πáya.
Digitization Notes: (2017‚Äì2021) Tyler Neill: Supplied missing _ArthƒÅpattipariccheda_ (5.8), restructured for PramƒÅ·πáa NLP collection.
(2025) Tyler Neill: Added page numbers for HANSEL structure, corrected about 150 unmetrical verses.
kumƒÅrilabha·π≠·π≠a_≈õlokavƒÅrtika.File Creation Method: I (Tyler Neill) produced this HANSEL edition by minimally adapting the corresponding file in the PramƒÅ·πáa NLP collection by adding page numbers. The PramƒÅ·πáa NLP file had been manually converted from Helmut Krasser's Word document into plain text, also by myself a few years earlier, mainly using regular expressions. Krasser's original Word file had been manually typed from the edition in an abstracted fashion, namely by extracting only the ≈ölokavƒÅrtika content and ignoring both PƒÅrthasƒÅrathi Mi≈õra's commentary and all physical aspects of the edition (e.g., page numbers). The text is known to contain errors and still needs a final proofread.
kumƒÅrilabha·π≠·π≠a_≈õlokavƒÅrtika.Word Division Style: Roman-like (ity evam, not ityevam), but inconsistent

Edition: Editors: Wilhelm Rau, Peri Sarveswara Sharma
Title: VaÃÑkyapadiÃÑyaprameyasa·πÉgraha: ein anonymes Scholion zum zweiten KaÃÑ·πá·∏ça des VaÃÑkyapadiÃÑya
Series: Abhandlungen der Marburger Gelehrten Gesellschaft
Extent: The edition contains a complete text of the known work.

vƒÅkyapadƒ´yaprameyasa·πÉgraha.Structure: The base text VƒÅkyakƒÅ·πá·∏ça comprises 493 kƒÅrikƒÅs. Of these, the Prameyasa·πÉgraha discusses 258 and leaves 235 untreated, according to Rau's marginal annotations The 258 kƒÅrikƒÅs discussed are 3, 5, 10, 12, 19‚Äì22, 30‚Äì31, 64‚Äì65, 69, 72‚Äì80, 87‚Äì88, 92, 95‚Äì101, 105, 108, 120‚Äì123, 154‚Äì162, 164‚Äì172, 174‚Äì176, 178‚Äì183, 185‚Äì190, 192‚Äì195, 197‚Äì202, 204, 216‚Äì217, 219‚Äì227, 229‚Äì230, 233, 238‚Äì258, 267, 273‚Äì281, 283‚Äì284, 298, 300, 302‚Äì308, 311‚Äì316, 318‚Äì321, 323‚Äì328, 334‚Äì335, 346‚Äì349, 351, 354‚Äì357, 359‚Äì364, 367, 369‚Äì376, 378‚Äì385, 388‚Äì397, 399‚Äì402, 404, 406, 409‚Äì434, 436, 438‚Äì440, 442‚Äì447, 449‚Äì450, 452, and 454‚Äì468. This coverage proceeds largely in linear order through the chapter, with only a few deviations from strict sequentiality. Contributor C. Li created headings for groups of kƒÅrikƒÅs discussed, which are retained here to provide the table of contents.
vƒÅkyapadƒ´yaprameyasa·πÉgraha.Work Description: The VƒÅkyapadƒ´ya-Prameyasa·πÉgraha is an anonymous scholastic work, probably composed in the early twelfth century, that builds upon Bhart·πõhari's VƒÅkyapadƒ´ya ‚Äî specifically about half the second chapter of the three chapters, called the VƒÅkyakƒÅ·πá·∏ça ‚Äî to create a compact doctrinal compendium. It survives in a single incomplete manuscript discovered in Benares in 1969, with the author's name lost along with the colophon.
Digitization Notes: (2016‚Äì2017) Charles Li: Typed up; removed brackets with folio/line numbers, e.g. (2r5:) for easier searching; added notes in brackets at the beginning of some paragraphs, indicating the pages being transcribed and also the verses being commented, e.g, "2,222" is 2nd kƒÅ·πá·∏ça, verse 222, which information is from Rau, in the margin notes.
(Jan 2026) Tyler Neill: Re-OCR (using two models, on 1981 edition, merged with AI), manual reconciliation of differences relative to previous version, some more light proofreading, markup for HANSEL.
vƒÅkyapadƒ´yaprameyasa·πÉgraha.File Creation Method: I (T. Neill) produced this HANSEL edition by first running a PDF of the 1981 edition through both Google Cloud Vision OCR (via Skrutable) and Gemini 2.0 Flash (via Dharmamitra). These two OCR outputs were then automatically harmonized using Gemini 2.5 Flash (via direct API and using a custom prompt). Finally, the harmonized OCR output was contrasted against the previous version by C. Li using Meld, and differences were reconciled individually with reference to the PDF. Additional structural markup into sections was prepared by C. Li on the basis of Rau's marginal annotations of VP kƒÅrikƒÅs. Brackets marking partly legible or uncertain readings were kept in an intermediate file but removed in the project edition, leaving only the readings and Rau‚Äôs ellipses. Rau‚Äôs empty brackets ([]) for missing material, whose extent he described in footnotes, were replaced with "...". The text still needs a final proofread.

Additional Files.description: full bracket information from edition, including mid-line hyphens

Additional Notes: Bronkhorst's review in Kratylos ([here on Archive](https://archive.org/details/bronkhorst_review_rau_1981)) contains useful perspective on the nature of the work and how to best use Rau's edition.

bƒÅ·πáa_kƒÅdambarƒ´.Title: KƒÅdambarƒ´
Edition: Note: Bombay Sanskrit Series No. XXIV was printed in three forms: 1883 (first edition), 1885 (revised edition), and 1889 (simple reset of 1885).
Extent: The edition contains a complete text.
The text fills 368 pages in the edition, with no other intervening material.
bƒÅ·πáa_kƒÅdambarƒ´.Work Description: This famous literary work ‚Äî a romance between two main characters CandrƒÅpƒ´·∏ça and KƒÅdambarƒ´, plus innumerable side stories ‚Äî was begun by BƒÅ·πáa Bha·π≠·π≠a (570‚Äì650 CE) and finished posthumously by his son Bh≈´·π£a·πáa Bha·π≠·π≠a (600‚Äì660 CE). These two contributions are the so-called P≈´rvabhƒÅga (Peterson ed. pp. 1‚Äì237) and UttarabhƒÅga (pp. 239‚Äì369), respectively. Peterson's edition, on which this e-text is based, added paragraph breaks as further structure. Later editions and translations also add headers to indicate beginnings of significant portions of the story, but no such headers are used here.
Digitization Notes: (2022-02-01) Andrew Ollett: Initial OCR (using Google Cloud Vision, on 1885/9 edition ‚Äî not 1883 as noted), some proofreading, XML markup for SARIT, uploaded to GitHub.

(2024‚Äì2025) Tyler Neill: Second OCR (using two models, on 1885 edition, merged with AI), manual reconciliation of differences relative to previous version, some more proofreading, markup for HANSEL.
bƒÅ·πáa_kƒÅdambarƒ´.File Creation Method: I (Tyler Neill) produced this HANSEL edition by first running a PDF of the 1885 edition through both Google Cloud Vision OCR (via Skrutable) and Gemini 2.0 Flash (via Dharmamitra). These two OCR outputs were then automatically harmonized using Gemini 2.5 Pro (via direct API and using a custom prompt). Finally, the harmonized OCR output was contrasted against the previous version by Andrew Ollett (which was in fact based on 1885/1889 as well) using Meld, and differences were reconciled individually with reference to the PDF. The text still needs a final proofread.

bhagavadajjuka.Title: Bhagavadajjuka
Edition: Title: Die Heiligen-Het√§re: Eine indische Yoga-Kom√∂die
Extent: The edition document contains a complete text.
The text fills 26 pages (evens between 8‚Äì58) in the edition, with no intervening material.
bhagavadajjuka.Structure: Play in one act, with a prologue (ƒÅmukha).  Location markers (page,line) designate the start of each speech block or in some cases, a stage direction.
bhagavadajjuka.Work Description: Bhagavadajjukam (‚ÄúThe Holy Man and the Harlot‚Äù) is a one-act farcical play (prahasana) preserved in a South Indian manuscript tradition and likely composed in the 6th or 7th century CE. Its authorship is uncertain: it is traditionally associated with BodhƒÅyana and has also been attributed to King Mahendravikramavarma Pallava, but neither ascription is secure. In the satirical plot, an ascetic, his pupil, and a courtesan (ajjukƒÅ) become caught up in a comic confusion of souls and bodies, poking fun at notions of religious discipline, intellectual discourse, and bodily identity. The Straube‚ÄìSteiner edition builds on the important edition of Achan (1925), going further through comparison with other editions and through careful normalization of orthography, punctuation, and Prakrit dialectical forms.
Translations: German, M. Straube & R. Steiner (eds.), plus U. Roesler, J. Soni, and L. Soni, 2006, ‚ÄúDie Heiligen-Het√§re‚Äù

English, M. Lockwood and A. Vishnu Bhat, 1994, ‚ÄúBhagavadajjuka prahasana: A Philosophical Farce by King Mahendravikramavarma Pallava‚Äù, [on Academia](https://www.academia.edu/12366085/Metatheater_and_Sanskrit_Drama_Part_II)
English, J. A. B. van Buitenen, 1971, ‚ÄúThe Hermit and the Harlot‚Äù
Italian, F. Belloni-Filippi, 1931/1959, ‚ÄúL‚Äôasceta trasmutato in etera di BodhƒÅyana‚Äù
bhagavadajjuka.Source Collection: Personal files of R. Steiner and M. Straube.
Digitization Notes: (Dec 2025 ‚Äì Jan 2026) Christian Ferstl: Manually prepared transcript of edition, modified punctuation, added some (not all) inter-word spacing, added Sanskrit chƒÅyƒÅs from Achan 1925.
(Jan 2026) Roland Steiner: Submitted plaintext file of edition with identical text to that printed in 2006.
bhagavadajjuka.File Creation Method: I (T. Neill) produced this HANSEL edition by first manually converting R. Steiner's and M. Straube's submitted plain-text file by adding basic structural markup (tabbed verses, stage directions, Prakrit). I then did the same for Christian Ferstl's manual transcript, which contained partial inter-word spacing (hyphenation within compounds was removed), Sanskrit chƒÅyƒÅs (supplied/adapted from Achan 1925), and a few corrections of typos. Next, I re-OCRed the PDF to recover line-break information, then merged this information into the structured transcript. Finally, I added structural markup for HANSEL and gave the text a full proofread, during which I checked word separation...)  For technical reasons, in cases where a line started with a combination stage direction and speaker identification, I have inverted the order to place the latter first and the former second (e.g. ‚Äúpravi≈õya vid≈´·π£aka·∏• |‚Äù becomes ‚Äúvid≈´·π£aka·∏• ‚Äî ((pravi≈õya))‚Äù.) Similarly, I have rendered the "nepathye" stage direction as speaker identification
Additional Notes: R. Steiner's philological commentary on the 2006 edition and translation is highly recommended. Find it [here on the publisher's website](http://www.kirchheimverlag.de/SteinerRoland_PhilologischeUntersuchungenBhagavadajjuka_2010.pdf).
